section.title: Wives for Benjamin
section.sectionRationale: The final chapter of Judges tries to bind the wound left by the civil war. Israel weeps that a whole tribe is nearly lost, yet they are trapped by a rash oath sworn at Mizpah never to give their daughters to Benjamin. To provide wives for the six hundred survivors they devise two grim schemes: they destroy Jabesh-gilead, which had failed to muster, and hand over its four hundred virgins; and for the rest they instruct the Benjamites to seize the dancing daughters of Shiloh at the yearly feast. The book then closes with its defining verdict: there was no king in Israel, and everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
section.thesis: Israel's grief is real and their desire to preserve a brother tribe is good, yet bound by a foolish oath and lacking a righteous king, they answer one atrocity with fresh violence and seizure, so that the book ends not in resolution but in a sober diagnosis, everyone did what was right in his own eyes, that leaves us aching for the true King.
section.frameNote: Four movements: Israel's grief and the trap of the rash oath, verses 1 to 7; the destruction of Jabesh-gilead for wives, verses 8 to 15; the seizing of the daughters of Shiloh, verses 16 to 24; and the book's closing verdict, verse 25.

units.label: Israel's grief and the rash oath

verses.text: Now the men of Israel had {{shaba|sworn an oath}} at Mizpah, saying, “Not one of us will give his daughter in marriage to a Benjamite.”
So the people came to Bethel and sat there before God until evening, lifting up their voices and weeping bitterly.
“Why, O LORD God of Israel,” they cried out, “has this happened in Israel? Today in Israel one tribe is missing!”
The next day the people got up early, built an altar there, and presented burnt offerings and peace offerings.

The Israelites asked, “Who among all the tribes of Israel did not come to the assembly before the LORD?” For they had taken a solemn oath that anyone who failed to come up before the LORD at Mizpah would surely be put to death.
And the Israelites grieved for their brothers, the Benjamites, and said, “Today a tribe is cut off from Israel.
What should we do about wives for the survivors, since we have sworn by the LORD not to give them our daughters in marriage?”
So they asked, “Which one of the tribes of Israel failed to come up before the LORD at Mizpah?” And, in fact, no one from Jabesh-gilead had come to the camp for the assembly.
For when the people were counted, none of the residents of Jabesh-gilead were there.
So the congregation sent 12,000 of their most valiant men and commanded them: “Go and put to the sword those living in Jabesh-gilead, including women and children.
This is what you are to do: {{cherem|Devote to destruction}} every male, as well as every female who has had relations with a man.”
So they found among the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead four hundred young women who had not had relations with a man, and they brought them to the camp at Shiloh in the land of Canaan.
Then the whole congregation sent a message of peace to the Benjamites who were at the rock of Rimmon.
And at that time the Benjamites returned and were given the women who were spared from Jabesh-gilead. But there were not enough women for all of them.
The people grieved for Benjamin, because the LORD had made a void in the tribes of Israel.

units.label: The seizing of the daughters of Shiloh

verses.text: Then the elders of the congregation said, “What should we do about wives for those who remain, since the women of Benjamin have been destroyed?”
They added, “There must be heirs for the survivors of Benjamin, so that a tribe of Israel will not be wiped out.
But we cannot give them our daughters as wives.” For the Israelites had sworn, “Cursed is he who gives a wife to a Benjamite.”
“But look,” they said, “there is a yearly feast to the LORD in Shiloh, which is north of Bethel east of the road that goes up from Bethel to Shechem, and south of Lebonah.”
So they commanded the Benjamites: “Go, hide in the vineyards

and watch. When you see the daughters of Shiloh come out to perform their dances, each of you is to come out of the vineyards, catch for himself a wife from the daughters of Shiloh, and go to the land of Benjamin.

When their fathers or brothers come to us to complain, we will tell them, ‘Do us a favor by helping them, since we did not get wives for each of them in the war. Since you did not actually give them your daughters, you have no guilt.’”
The Benjamites did as instructed and carried away the number of women they needed from the dancers they caught. They went back to their own inheritance, rebuilt their cities, and settled in them.
And at that time, each of the Israelites returned from there to his own tribe and clan, each to his own inheritance.

units.label: The closing verdict
units.note: everyone did what was right in his own eyes

verses.text: In those days there was no {{melek|king}} in Israel; everyone did what was {{yashar|right}} in his own eyes.

shaba.gloss: To swear, take an oath, bind oneself by a vow. Israel's rash oath at Mizpah, never to give daughters to Benjamin, drives the whole chapter's crisis. The word exposes the danger of vows made in the heat of zeal; bound by their own words, the people scramble for loopholes rather than repent of a foolish oath, and the binding leads to more bloodshed.

cherem.gloss: The ban, devotion to destruction, the giving over of persons or things wholly to the LORD, usually by their destruction. It belonged to God's own judgments on the Canaanites. Here Israel turns it on a town of their own, Jabesh-gilead, to extract wives; the misuse of a sacred category to solve a self-made crisis shows how far the people have drifted from God's ways.

melek.gloss: King. The book's closing verdict, there was no king in Israel, names the vacuum behind every horror of the epilogue. It is not merely a call for any monarchy but for righteous rule under God; the phrase leaves the reader leaning forward to the coming of a true and righteous King, David in shadow and Christ in fullness.

yashar.gloss: Right, straight, upright. The final clause, everyone did what was yashar in his own eyes, is the book's diagnosis of the whole era. What is truly right belongs to God's eyes, not ours; when each person becomes his own measure of right, the result is the chaos and cruelty Judges has just displayed. The phrase indicts self-rule and cries out for the King who does what is right in the LORD's eyes.

basic.context: After the war, Israel weeps that Benjamin is nearly extinct, but they are trapped by an oath they had sworn at Mizpah never to give their daughters to Benjamin. Determined to keep a tribe alive without breaking their word, they find two grim solutions. First, they discover that Jabesh-gilead had not joined the assembly, so they destroy the town under the ban and spare four hundred virgins as wives for Benjamin. When these are not enough for the six hundred survivors, they tell the remaining Benjamites to hide in the vineyards at Shiloh and seize wives from the young women who come out to dance at the yearly feast, arranging in advance to smooth over the fathers' complaints. Benjamin is preserved, the people go home, and the book ends with its haunting verdict: there was no king in Israel, and everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
basic.hermeneutics: This chapter describes the tangled, sinful schemes of a people bound by a rash oath and lacking a righteous king; it does not commend them. Their grief for Benjamin is genuine and their concern to preserve a tribe is understandable, but the means, another massacre and the abduction of the daughters of Shiloh, are laid before us as further evidence of a nation gone morally astray. The closing verse is the interpretive key: everything here is what happens when everyone does what is right in his own eyes.
basic.originalLanguages: The crisis flows from an oath, shaba, sworn in zeal at Mizpah, which the people now cannot escape. To gain wives they place Jabesh-gilead under the cherem, the ban of destruction meant for God's judgments, now turned against their own. And the book ends by naming the age: there was no melek, no king, and everyone did what was yashar, right, in his own eyes, when true right belongs to God's eyes alone.
basic.typology: The whole chapter is a portrait of what is missing. The absence of a righteous king who could lead the people in wisdom and mercy leaves them stumbling from atrocity to atrocity. The book thus becomes a long, aching prayer for a King, answered first in David and finally in Christ, the King who does what is right in the LORD's eyes and rescues rather than seizes His bride.
basic.christ: Judges ends on its deepest need, a king who does what is right in God's eyes rather than his own. Israel could not save itself from the chaos of self-rule, and every human scheme only deepened the wreckage. This is the soil in which the promise of the King grows. Christ is the true King who came not to seize wives by violence but to win His bride by love and sacrifice, and who does perfectly what is right in the eyes of His Father. The dark close of Judges makes the coming of that King shine all the brighter.
basic.god: God is grieved over and present in this chapter, even where the people act foolishly; it is said that the LORD had made a void in the tribes, and the people cry out to Him and worship. Yet God commands none of these schemes; they are Israel's own devising. He is the God whose ways the people have abandoned, the God whose righteous rule they desperately need, and the God who, over the wreckage of the judges, is quietly preparing to give His people a king, and beyond every king, His Son.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 8 records Israel finally asking for a king. 1 Samuel 11 shows Jabesh-gilead rescued by Saul, tying this town to the coming monarchy. Judges 17:6 first sounds the refrain about no king. And John 18 shows Christ the King whose kingdom is not of this world, doing His Father's will.
basic.oneStory: The book of Judges ends not with rescue but with diagnosis: there was no king, and everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Israel's desperate, violent schemes to save Benjamin show a people unable to heal themselves. The whole downward spiral leaves us longing for the righteous King, given in part through David and in fullness in Christ, who does what is right in God's eyes and wins His people not by seizure but by self-giving love.

context.literary: The closing scene of the epilogue and of the whole book. It resolves the plot, Benjamin survives, but withholds any moral resolution, ending instead on the refrain that has run through chapters 17 to 21. The two schemes mirror and escalate the book's disorder: a fresh massacre and an organized abduction, both to undo the effects of a rash oath. The final verse, the fullest form of the refrain, is the narrator's last word and the interpretive lens for everything from chapter 17 onward.
context.historical: Oaths sworn before the LORD were binding and dangerous to break, which is why Israel schemes around rather than through the vow. Jabesh-gilead lay east of the Jordan; its later rescue by Saul, a Benjamite, in 1 Samuel 11 creates a poignant link, for the wives of Benjamin came in part from Jabesh. The yearly feast at Shiloh, where the tabernacle stood, involved young women dancing in the vineyards, a joyful setting turned into the scene of an abduction. Marriage by capture was a known practice in the ancient world, but the narrative frames it as a symptom of disorder, not a norm to admire.
context.canonical: This chapter closes the era of the judges and prepares directly for the books of Samuel and the rise of the monarchy. The refrain there was no king points forward to Saul, David, and ultimately to the messianic King. Ruth, set in the same period, offers a quiet counter-story of covenant faithfulness and leads to David, showing that God was preserving a righteous line even in this dark age. The whole trajectory runs to Christ, the King in whose eyes alone true right is found.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that even Israel's attempt to repair the damage of the civil war, undertaken with real grief, collapses into further violence and seizure because the people are bound by a rash oath and have no righteous king, and to seal the book with the verdict that everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the schemes of a disordered people; it prescribes none of them. The destruction of Jabesh-gilead and the abduction of the daughters of Shiloh are symptoms of the age, not models. What endures is the danger of rash vows, the folly of self-rule, the seriousness of God's grief over His divided people, and the need for a righteous King who does what is right in God's eyes.

debates.question: Should Israel have kept the rash oath at all costs, or repented of it?

views.label: Bound to keep the oath
views.case: Vows sworn before the LORD were solemn and not to be broken lightly, Numbers 30; the people honored the oath's letter and sought a way to preserve Benjamin without technically giving their daughters, showing a real, if misguided, reverence for their word.

views.label: The oath was rash and should have been repented of
views.case: The vow was made in the heat of zeal and led to fresh bloodshed and abduction; Scripture warns against rash vows, Ecclesiastes 5:2-6, and true wisdom would have been to grieve the foolish oath before God rather than commit new atrocities to keep it.

debates.bearing: Either way the episode warns us to weigh our words before God and exposes how self-made bindings, held without wisdom, can drive good people into deeper evil; it magnifies the need for a King who leads in true wisdom.

debates.question: How does the closing verse, there was no king, function?

views.label: A call for the monarchy
views.case: The refrain prepares for Samuel and the rise of kingship; a righteous king could have administered justice, restrained the mob, and spared both Jabesh-gilead and the daughters of Shiloh, so the verse reads as an argument that Israel needs a king.

views.label: A call for God's righteous rule, fulfilled in Christ
views.case: The deeper problem is not the mere lack of a monarch but that everyone did what was right in his own eyes rather than God's; even Israel's later kings often failed, so the verse ultimately points beyond David to the King who alone does what is right in the LORD's eyes, Christ.

debates.bearing: Both hold together: the verse looks ahead to David and, through and beyond him, to Christ, teaching that only rule submitted to God's eyes, not our own, can heal a people bent on self-rule.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's whole crisis springs from an oath, shaba: Israel had sworn at Mizpah never to give their daughters to Benjamin, and now, bound by their own words, they scheme for loopholes rather than repent of a rash vow, and the binding drives them to more blood. To supply wives they place Jabesh-gilead under the cherem, the ban of utter destruction that belonged to God's own judgments on the Canaanites, now turned against a town of Israel to solve a self-made problem, a sacred category twisted for human ends. And then the book seals itself with its most famous words: in those days there was no melek, no king, in Israel; everyone did what was yashar, right, in his own eyes. The tragedy of the whole book is compressed into that last phrase, for true right belongs to God's eyes, not ours. When each person becomes his own measure of right, the outcome is the chaos, cruelty, and self-deception that Judges has laid bare. The vocabulary itself, oath, ban, king, right, preaches the lesson: a people ruled by its own eyes and its own vows needs a King who does what is right in the eyes of the LORD.

typology.type: The absent righteous king behind the chaos
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the King who does what is right in the eyes of the LORD and leads His people in wisdom and mercy, John 8:29 and Revelation 19:11.
God's faithfulness to preserve His people and their tribes toward the coming of the King; from Benjamin later comes Saul, and God keeps the whole nation for the Messiah, Romans 11:1-5.

typology.type: Wives seized by force to secure a future
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Bridegroom, who does not seize but gives Himself to win and cleanse His bride, Ephesians 5:25-27.

study.christ: Judges ends by naming its own deepest need: a king who does what is right in God's eyes rather than his own. Israel could not save itself from the chaos of self-rule, and every scheme to repair the damage only spread the ruin, a town destroyed, daughters carried off. This is the barren soil in which the hope of the King takes root. Christ is that King. He does perfectly what is right in the eyes of His Father, John 8:29, for He came not to do His own will but the will of the One who sent Him. He is the true Bridegroom who wins His bride not by ambush and seizure, as Benjamin took the daughters of Shiloh, but by laying down His life to make her His own, Ephesians 5:25. The darker the last chapter of Judges, the brighter shines the promise of the King who was coming, first faintly in David, then fully in Jesus, to do what is right and to heal what self-rule had broken.
study.god: God is not the author of these schemes, yet He is not absent from the chapter. The people cry out to Him, worship, and build an altar; it is said that the LORD had made a void in the tribes, so that even the tragedy is under His hand. He is the God whose righteous ways Israel has abandoned, whose eyes define what is truly right, and whose rule the people so desperately need. Over the wreckage of the judges He is quietly at work, preserving even Benjamin, keeping a people through whom the King will come. The closing verdict is finally an invitation: to stop trusting our own eyes and to long for, and submit to, the God who rules in righteousness through His Son.

crossReferences.note: The first sounding of the refrain, everyone did what was right in his own eyes, which the book's final verse completes.
Saul the Benjamite rescues Jabesh-gilead, linking this town, source of Benjamin's wives, to the coming monarchy.
The warning against rash vows before God, the very folly that binds Israel into fresh violence here.
Christ, the true Bridegroom, gives Himself to win and cleanse His bride, the opposite of wives seized by force.

study.oneStory: The book of Judges ends not with a rescue but with a diagnosis: there was no king, and everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Israel's grief for Benjamin is real, but its violent schemes to save the tribe only reveal a people unable to heal themselves. The whole spiral of the epilogue, atrocity, civil war, and now massacre and abduction, leaves us leaning forward with longing for the righteous King. That longing is answered in part through David and in fullness in Christ, the King who does what is right in God's eyes and wins His people not by seizure but by self-giving love.

quiz.q: What trapped Israel as they tried to provide wives for the surviving Benjamites?

options.text: A rash oath they had sworn at Mizpah never to give their daughters to Benjamin.
options.note: Verses 1 and 7 show the vow that drives all the scheming that follows.

options.text: A famine that had left no families willing to marry.
options.note: There was no famine here; the obstacle was their own oath.

options.text: A command from God forbidding any marriage.
options.note: God gave no such command; it was Israel's own oath, not a divine word.

options.text: A law that Benjamites could never marry at all.
options.note: No such law existed; the barrier was the self-imposed oath.

quiz.q: How did Israel first obtain wives for the six hundred survivors of Benjamin?

options.text: They destroyed Jabesh-gilead, which had not joined the assembly, and spared its four hundred virgins.
options.note: Verses 8 to 12 record the massacre and the taking of the young women.

options.text: They bought wives from the surrounding nations.
options.note: They did not buy foreign wives; they destroyed an Israelite town to supply them.

options.text: They persuaded fathers to willingly give their daughters.
options.note: The oath forbade giving daughters; the wives came by violence and seizure, not willing gift.

options.text: God miraculously provided wives for them.
options.note: These were Israel's own grim schemes, not a miracle from God.

quiz.q: How were the remaining wives obtained from Shiloh?

options.text: The Benjamites hid in the vineyards and seized the young women who came out to dance at the yearly feast.
options.note: Verses 20 to 23 describe the arranged abduction of the daughters of Shiloh.

options.text: The elders of Shiloh gladly offered their daughters as a gift.
options.note: The scheme was designed precisely because the daughters could not be freely given; they were seized.

options.text: The women of Shiloh volunteered to marry the Benjamites.
options.note: There is no hint of volunteering; the women were caught while dancing.

options.text: Lots were cast to assign wives fairly.
options.note: No lots were cast for this; the women were taken by force from the dance.

quiz.q: What is the closing verse and verdict of the book of Judges?

options.text: In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
options.note: Verse 25 is the book's defining diagnosis of the whole era.

options.text: The land had rest, and the people served the LORD faithfully.
options.note: The opposite; the book ends in disorder, not faithful rest.

options.text: The LORD raised up a judge to deliver Israel.
options.note: That is the earlier cycle, but the book ends on the lack of a king, not a new judge.

options.text: Israel crowned David as king over all the tribes.
options.note: David comes much later; Judges ends by noting there was no king at all.

quiz.q: What does the closing verdict, everyone did what was right in his own eyes, teach us?

options.text: That self-rule, making ourselves the measure of right, ends in chaos, and that we need a King who does what is right in God's eyes.
options.note: The refrain diagnoses the era and points beyond David to Christ, the righteous King.

options.text: That people should always trust their own conscience above all else.
options.note: The opposite; the verse condemns making our own eyes the measure of right.

options.text: That any king, good or bad, would have solved every problem.
options.note: The need is not merely a monarch but righteous rule under God, fulfilled in Christ.

options.text: That God had abandoned Israel entirely.
options.note: God was still at work preserving His people toward the coming King.

questions: Israel was bound by a rash oath and scrambled for loopholes rather than repent of it. Where have your own hasty words or commitments boxed you in, and what would it look like to bring them honestly to God?
The people grieved sincerely for Benjamin yet answered the crisis with fresh violence. How can real compassion still go badly wrong when it is not guided by God's wisdom?
The book ends by naming the age: everyone did what was right in his own eyes. Where are you tempted to make your own eyes, rather than God's, the measure of what is right?
Judges closes with a longing for a righteous king it never quite receives. How does the coming of Christ, the King who does what is right in God's eyes, answer the ache this book leaves in you?
Where Benjamin seized wives by force, Christ wins His bride by laying down His life. How does the self-giving love of the true Bridegroom reshape the way you love and lead those entrusted to you?

translationNotes.note: Devote to destruction renders the Hebrew cherem, the ban; the BSB footnote explains that forms of cherem refer to giving persons or things wholly over to the LORD, whether by destruction or as an offering. Here the sacred category is turned against an Israelite town.
The yearly feast to the LORD at Shiloh, where the tabernacle stood, is carefully located by the narrator; a joyful festival with dancing becomes the setting for the seizing of wives.

There was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes repeats and completes the refrain of 17:6 and 18:1 and 19:1, sealing the whole book. The Hebrew yashar, right or upright, sets each person's own judgment against what is right in the eyes of the LORD.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 21:1-25, all 25 verses. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. The schemes are handled soberly as description not prescription, with the closing verdict framing the whole. Sermon set to none; no message from a trusted cluster was verified this session.
